skill.name: ios-application-dev
skill.description: Guidance for building native iOS applications with UIKit, SnapKit, SwiftUI, Metal, accessibility, navigation, layout, and system integration patterns.
skill.summary: Build and review native iOS apps using Apple platform conventions.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, sensitive data, and network indicators. Review found the command, weak crypto, reconnaissance, and sensitive data alerts are documentation false positives from Markdown backticks, Swift syntax, headings, and UI examples; the only network evidence is example.com URLSession sample code. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, executable scripts, or real command runners were found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command detections come from Markdown inline code, Swift code fences, API names, and reference tables. No shell, Ruby, process execution, or command runner instructions were found in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation tables or Swift examples using backticks for formatting. Targeted review found no bash, shell, Ruby, Process, NSTask, system, eval, or exec usage.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Illustrative Examples
low_findings.description: The network findings are sample Swift URLSession snippets that use https://api.example.com. They do not reference a real collection endpoint and do not send secrets or environment data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations use the reserved example.com domain inside instructional Swift code. No credential handling or exfiltration behavior is present near the URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto, Sensitive File, and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography detections match words in headings such as Design or CRITICAL, sensitive findings match UI password or keyboard examples, and reconnaissance findings match system UI APIs. These are not security operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative flagged lines are ordinary iOS guidance and SwiftUI controls. No cryptographic primitives, certificate files, host enumeration, or system reconnaissance logic was found.

content.value_statement: iOS teams need consistent platform guidance for layout, navigation, accessibility, and Swift code quality. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused references for building and reviewing UIKit and SwiftUI apps.
actual_capabilities: Guides UIKit, SnapKit, and SwiftUI layout choices for native iOS screens.
Reviews touch targets, safe areas, Dynamic Type, Dark Mode, and VoiceOver support.
Explains navigation patterns with tab bars, navigation stacks, sheets, alerts, and context menus.
Provides Swift coding standards for optionals, protocols, concurrency, errors, ARC, and testing.
Covers system integration topics such as permissions, location, sharing, haptics, app lifecycle, and Spotlight.
Includes Metal shader and graphics guidance for animation, gradients, shape layers, and GPU-oriented rendering.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not run Xcode, build apps, or execute tests.
Examples are general guidance and must be adapted to each project architecture.
It does not replace current Apple documentation for newly released APIs.
It cannot verify App Store review compliance without project-specific review.

use_cases.title: Design an iOS Screen
use_cases.description: Plan a SwiftUI or UIKit screen with safe areas, Dynamic Type, touch targets, and platform navigation patterns.

use_cases.title: Review an iOS Pull Request
use_cases.description: Check Swift code and interface changes against Apple conventions, accessibility requirements, and maintainability standards.

use_cases.title: Modernize Legacy UIKit
use_cases.description: Convert older view controller patterns toward compositional layout, modern buttons, semantic colors, and structured navigation.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic iOS Layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the iOS application development skill to review this screen for safe areas, 44 point touch targets, spacing, and Dynamic Type support.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a SwiftUI Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the SwiftUI guidance to propose the view structure, navigation pattern, state ownership, accessibility labels, and empty states for this feature.

prompt_templates.title: Audit UIKit Navigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the navigation and UIKit references to audit this flow for tab usage, modal presentation, back gestures, alerts, and state restoration.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Swift Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Swift coding standards, accessibility, system integration, and graphics references to review this iOS code for correctness, maintainability, privacy, performance, and release risk.

output_examples.input: Review a profile edit screen for iOS quality.
output: The review flags small tap targets on icon buttons and missing VoiceOver labels.
It recommends semantic colors, Dynamic Type text styles, and a cancel path for the edit sheet.
It notes that destructive account actions should use confirmation and a destructive role.

output_examples.input: Plan navigation for a shopping app.
output: The plan uses a tab bar for Home, Search, Cart, and Account.
Product details use navigation push behavior, while checkout uses a focused sheet or full-screen flow.
The plan preserves tab state and keeps the back gesture available.

output_examples.input: Improve Swift networking and error handling guidance.
output: The response recommends typed errors, async functions, clear loading states, and user-facing recovery messages.
It avoids force unwraps and suggests structured decoding and cancellation-aware task handling.

best_practices: Provide the target iOS version, UI framework, and screen context before asking for implementation guidance.
Ask for accessibility, Dynamic Type, Dark Mode, and privacy checks as part of every UI review.
Use the reference files for focused reviews of navigation, layout, Swift style, graphics, or system integration.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to generate code without describing product behavior, data states, and navigation context.
Do not treat illustrative snippets as drop-in production code without project review and testing.
Do not ignore current Apple documentation when using newly released platform APIs.

faq.question: Does this skill write complete iOS apps?
faq.answer: It provides implementation guidance and review criteria, but it does not build, run, or test an app by itself.

faq.question: Can it help with both SwiftUI and UIKit?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes SwiftUI design guidance, UIKit component patterns, SnapKit layout notes, and navigation references.

faq.question: Does it cover accessibility?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Dynamic Type, semantic colors, VoiceOver labels, motion settings, contrast, and accessible interactions.

faq.question: Can it review Swift code quality?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for optionals, naming, protocols, async and await, error handling, ARC, testing, and security basics.

faq.question: Does it include graphics or Metal guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes references for Core Animation, shape layers, gradients, Metal shaders, and Apple GPU performance concepts.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The security review found documentation false positives and no malicious code, scripts, command execution, or prompt injection.
